sanskrit_devanagari:
योगी युञ्जीत सततमात्मानं रहसि स्थितः ।
एकाकी यतचित्तात्मा निराशीरपरिग्रहः ॥ १० ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yogī yuñjīta satatam ātmānaṁ rahasi sthitaḥ |
ekākī yata-cittātmā nirāśīr aparigrahaḥ || 10 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: A transcendentalist should always engage his body, mind and self in relationship with the Supreme; he should live alone in a secluded place and should always carefully control his mind. He should be free from desires and feelings of possessiveness.

A transcendentalist should always engage his body, mind and self in relationship with the Supreme; he should live alone in a secluded place and should always carefully control his mind. He should be free from desires and feelings of possessiveness.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 2896 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Kṛṣṇa is realized in different degrees as Brahman, Paramātmā and the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Kṛṣṇa consciousness means, concisely, to be always engaged in the transcendental loving service of the Lord.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.10 A yogi, a man of meditation; satatam yunjita, should constantly concentrate; atmanam, his mind; sthitah, by staying; rahasi, in a solitary place, in mountain caves etc.; ekaki, alone, without any companion; yata-citta-atma, with mind and body controlled; nirasih, without expectations,…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-10-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.10 Let the yogi constantly concentrate his mind by remaining in seclusion, alone, with body and mind controlled, free from desires, and without possessions.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.10 The Yogin who is steady in the practice of Karma Yoga, should ‘constantly’, i.e., daily when practising Yoga, fix his mind to the practice of Yoga, i.e., make himself engaged in the vision of the self. He must remain in a ‘solitary place,’ i.e., a place devoid of crowd and noise.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-10-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.10 The Yogin should constantly fix his mind on the Self, dwelling alone in some secluded place, with mind and body controlled, free from longings and from the sense of mineness over possessions.

positions.rendering: remaining in seclusion, alone — physical and mental solitude both
dwelling alone in some secluded place
live alone in a secluded place

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Translators agree this is the somatic precondition for sustained meditation. The verse's literal claim is solitude + aloneness; engineering analog (deep-work setup, focus blocks) is approximate, not identical.

literal_meaning: Let the yogi constantly yoke the self, dwelling in solitude, alone, with mind and body controlled, free of expectations, free of possessions.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna pivots from disposition to practice. The yogi is to constantly yoke the self — in solitude, alone, with controlled mind and body, without expectations, without possessions. The verse opens the meditation-practice block (6.10-6.32). The four conditions — solitude, aloneness, mind-body control, freedom from expectation/possession — are preconditions for the somatic instruction that follows.
